---
title: "Reading roadmap"
description: "Ways through the book, chapter maturity, and just-in-time supporting material."
page-role: frontmatter
---

The default route follows the parts in order, from a single measured voltage to a
qualified system. It is the safest route for a new reader because later chapters
assume the measurement habits, reference directions, model limits, and evidence
practices established in Part I [@jcgm2008gum]. A route is a way to emphasize a
destination, not permission to ignore prerequisites: this page tells you where you
may enter, what each part hands to the next, and how to turn reading into
evidence.

The book is fifty-five chapters in seven parts and two appendix families. Each
part answers one central question and carries a canonical reference alongside the
prose, so that the compact models in the text can always be traced back to a
primary source rather than restated from memory.

## The book at a glance

| Part | Chapters | Central question | Reference spine |
|---|---|---|---|
| [I — Electrical foundations](01-foundations/index.qmd) | F01–F08 | What are voltage, current, resistance, energy, and a *trustworthy* measurement? | @horowitz2015art; @bipm2019si; @jcgm2008gum |
| [II — Devices and analog](02-analog/index.qmd) | A01–A08 | How do physical devices shape and amplify real signals? | @sedra2020microelectronic |
| [III — Digital hardware](03-digital/index.qmd) | D01–D06 | How are values, decisions, state, and computation built from those circuits? | @harris2021digital |
| [IV — Embedded systems](04-embedded/index.qmd) | E01–E06 | How does software meet time, energy, peripherals, and failure? | @harris2021digital |
| [V — System domains](05-domains/index.qmd) | S01–S10 | How do we sample, process, control, power, and communicate? | @oppenheim1996signals; @astrom2021feedback |
| [VI — Product realization](06-realization/index.qmd) | R01–R06 | How does a design become reproducible, compliant, and maintainable hardware? | @ott2009electromagnetic |
| [VII — Complete systems](07-systems/index.qmd) | X01–X05 | How are cross-domain trade-offs verified and handed to another person? | *(integrates all of the above)* |
| [Appendices](appendices/index.qmd) | M01–M03, T01–T03 | What mathematics and working practice does a chapter reach for? | @bipm2019si; @jcgm2008gum |

Each part builds a durable capability rather than a topic list. Foundations make
measurement trustworthy; devices and analog make signals controllable; digital
and embedded make behavior computable and time-aware; the domains extend the same
models to sampling, control, power, and radio; realization makes a design
reproducible; and the final systems demand that every earlier detail survive
contact with a real requirement.

## Choose a destination

Pick the nearest destination to what you want to build. *Begin with* names the
non-negotiable entry material; *then emphasize* names the chapters that carry the
destination; *demonstrate readiness with* names the evidence that shows you
arrived. Every route still respects the chapter prerequisites recorded in
[`chapters.toml`](chapters.toml).

| Destination | Begin with | Then emphasize | Demonstrate readiness with |
|---|---|---|---|
| First trustworthy bench investigation | [safe practice](01-foundations/f01-safe-practice.qmd) (F01) | F02–F06 | a prediction, a current-limited test, an uncertainty statement, and a bounded conclusion |
| Analog instrument | Part I | A01–A06, then S01–S02 and R05 | a calibrated signal chain with a stated error budget [@sedra2020microelectronic] |
| Digital and embedded system | F01–F07, A01–A02 | D01–D06, E01–E05 | timed interface evidence, a recovered fault, and reproducible firmware [@harris2021digital] |
| Custom electronic product | Parts I–IV | R01–R06 alongside the relevant domain (S-part) chapters | a reviewed design baseline, staged bring-up, a verification matrix, and a release package [@ott2009electromagnetic] |
| Complete field system | the full route or equivalent demonstrated knowledge | X01–X05 | qualification evidence that another person can reproduce and maintain |

Institutional course mappings provide *paced* routes through these same chapter
IDs; see [Follow a paced institutional route](#follow-a-paced-institutional-route)
below. The table above is for a reader selecting a destination, not a semester.

## Read in dependency order, not just page order

Page order is one valid ordering of the dependency graph; it is not the only one.
A reader who already knows basic circuit theory can enter later without breaking a
dependency, provided every listed prerequisite is genuinely held. Three chains
carry most of the book and are worth seeing explicitly:

- **The measurement spine** — F01 → F02 → F03 → F06 — reaches a *trustworthy
  measurement* early, and almost every later chapter quietly assumes it. Do not
  skip it to reach a device chapter faster.
- **The dynamics spine** — F04 → F07 → F08 → S01 — carries stored energy,
  transients, impedance, and frequency response into signals and systems
  [@oppenheim1996signals]; it is the prerequisite for conversion (S02), control
  (S04), and RF (S07).
- **The realization spine** — R01 → R02 → R03/R04 → R05 → R06 — turns a working
  breadboard into a reproducible, compliant product, and it depends on both the
  analog (A04) and digital (D03) sides meeting at a schematic.

Two dependencies routinely surprise readers because they cross parts. A digital
chapter (D01) depends on the transistor as a switch (A02), because a logic level
is an analog quantity before it is a bit. And the embedded software chapters
depend on both the C-and-hardware boundary (E01) *and* digital timing (D03),
because a missed deadline is a timing failure, not a matter of coding style. When
in doubt, follow the prerequisite links at the head of a chapter rather than the
page order.

## Use the appendices just in time

The appendices are working references, not a final hurdle. Follow a prerequisite
link to them the moment a chapter first needs the technique, then return:

- **M01** — algebra, units, graphs, and complex quantities. Underpins F03 and the
  phasor notation of F08, and fixes the SI conventions used throughout the book
  [@bipm2019si].
- **M02** — calculus, differential equations, and linear algebra. Needed for
  transients (F07), signals (S01), and control (S04).
- **M03** — probability, statistics, and numerical decisions. Needed for
  uncertainty and debugging (F06) and for reliability and production test (R05)
  [@jcgm2008gum].
- **T01** — reading datasheets, standards, and judging source quality. Enters at
  requirements (R01) and wherever a claim rests on a datasheet or a standard.
- **T02** — tools, files, automation, and version control. Needed before HDL
  verification (D04) and the C boundary (E01).
- **T03** — records another person can reproduce and audit. The habit behind every
  project deliverable and the whole final part.

## Practice and project spine

Book chapters develop durable models. **Labs** ask for physical or authentic
evidence; **projects** force several models and interfaces to succeed together.
Their stable IDs let a class or an independent learner change apparatus without
rewriting the theory the ID points at.

### Labs: evidence goals (L01–L20)

| Stage | Evidence sequence | Integrating result |
|---|---|---|
| Foundations | L01–L05: safe instruments, loaded networks, power and heat, transients, and frequency response | **M1** — a qualified energy-and-light measurement claim |
| Devices and computation | L06–L10: device regions, amplifier limits, sensor calibration, logic timing, and HDL/FPGA evidence | **M2–M3** — a calibrated environmental monitor and a precision instrument |
| Embedded and domains | L11–L18: peripheral drivers, protocol faults, feedback control, protected power, PCB bring-up, converter error budgets, a legal radio link, and fault recovery | **M4–M6** — a resilient data logger, a secure node, and a field pilot |
| Realization and handover | L19–L20: production test, repeat-build, and field-readiness evidence | **M7–M8** — risk retirement, qualification, and reproducible handover |

The lab IDs denote **evidence goals, not permission to perform an activity**. Each
lab carries an explicit safety level: L01–L12 and L16/L18 are current-limited
extra-low-voltage work (L1), while L13–L15, L17, and L19–L20 involve controlled
energy, mechanical, or radio hazards (L2). The safety level, apparatus, stop
conditions, and local authorization must be defined *before* a lab becomes
deliverable.

### Projects: milestone integrations (M1–M8)

Each project is a milestone that binds a group of chapters into one accepted
result. They compound: a later milestone reuses the qualified evidence of the
earlier ones rather than rebuilding it.

| Project | Integrates | Accepted result |
|---|---|---|
| **M1** | F01–F06, T03 | a safe measurement plan, calibrated observations, and a qualified energy claim |
| **M2** | F07–F08, A01–A02, A08, E01, R01 | a two-variable monitor with protection, calibration, and a defensible use context |
| **M3** | A03–A06, S01–S02, R05, X01 | a measured signal chain with calibration and an uncertainty statement |
| **M4** | E02–E04, S04–S05, R02–R04, X02 | a fabrication-ready board, staged bring-up, tested firmware, and a release package |
| **M5** | D05–D06, E05, S02, S07, R05–R06, X03 | an alpha product with a threat model, a measured link, stress evidence, and recovery |
| **M6** | E06, S03, S06, S08–S10, R01, R06, X04 | a field pilot with ethical requirements, rollback, a service plan, and bounded claims |
| **M7** | R01, R05–R06, X05, T01, T03 | reviewed requirements, architecture, risk prototypes, and a qualification plan |
| **M8** | R04–R06, X05, T03 | a verified product, a reproducible dossier, a technical defense, and an accepted handover |

## Follow a paced institutional route

For a reader who wants a calendar rather than a destination, the same chapter,
lab, and project IDs are arranged into a reference degree program: an eight-
semester, 240-credit *Electronics Systems Engineering* route that introduces,
reinforces, and finally masters each chapter across courses. Its themes trace the
same elements-to-systems arc as the book:

| Semester | Theme | Milestone |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Observe, measure, model | M1 |
| 2 | Dynamic circuits and active devices | M2 |
| 3 | Analog, digital, signals, and metrology | M3 |
| 4 | Integrated interfaces and first PCB | M4 |
| 5 | Robust connected products | M5 |
| 6 | Advanced domains and field engineering | M6 |
| 7 | Specialize and validate | M7 |
| 8 | Qualify, transfer, and reflect | M8 |

There are no course-specific copies of the theory: a concept used by three courses
is still written once, and each course points back to the shared chapter ID. The
full course catalogue, prerequisites, and mastery levels are published with the
[reference program](../courses/README.md).

## Interpret chapter status

Each chapter reports its own maturity in its front matter. Status describes how
much of the chapter you can rely on today, not how important the chapter is.

| Status | What the reader can rely on |
|---|---|
| `outline` | scope, central question, concept sequence, and dependency boundary |
| `draft` | a complete instructional path, still subject to technical and editorial revision |
| `review` | worked results and evidence checked; review findings may still change the chapter |
| `released` | accepted for the stated edition and still bounded by its documented assumptions |

This is a development edition: the table of contents and the dependency structure
are complete, but the chapters are currently outlines. An outline is a scope
commitment, not yet a teachable or safety-authorizing chapter. Regardless of
status, prose in this book does not authorize work beyond the stated safety
boundary — reading a chapter is never authorization for mains, high-voltage,
high-energy battery, RF power, rotating-machine, medical, or safety-critical work.

## The reference spine

The book cites primary and canonical sources rather than restating them, and each
part builds against one:

- practical, bench-first electronics runs through the whole book
  [@horowitz2015art];
- device behavior follows the standard microelectronics treatment
  [@sedra2020microelectronic];
- logic, memory, and processors build toward computer architecture
  [@harris2021digital];
- sampling and signal processing follow the standard signals-and-systems text
  [@oppenheim1996signals];
- feedback and control draw on modern feedback-systems theory
  [@astrom2021feedback];
- realization takes electromagnetic compatibility as a design constraint, not a
  final test [@ott2009electromagnetic];
- and units and uncertainty are held to their defining references throughout — the
  SI [@bipm2019si] and the guide to expressing measurement uncertainty
  [@jcgm2008gum].

None of these is a substitute for a datasheet, a standard, or supervised
laboratory training, and neither is this book. The full
[bibliography](references.qmd) grows with the technical content it supports.

## A productive chapter cycle

Whichever route you take, read each chapter the same way.

Before reading, state a directional prediction and the evidence that might change
your mind. While reading, track the physical system, the schematic or
architecture, the equations, the assumptions, and the test points as one model
[@horowitz2015art]. At the end, recompute the central result, explain a plausible
mismatch, and state the claim that the available evidence supports—no stronger.
That cycle rides on an evidence ladder that runs from a rough estimate to a
qualified claim:

```text
estimate → calculation → simulation → measurement → repeated test
         → independent reproduction → field evidence → qualified claim
```

Not every question needs the top rung. The consequence of being wrong sets how
much evidence is appropriate: a sign convention can rest on a calculation, while a
safety or lifecycle claim cannot. Choosing the right rung—and stopping there—is
itself part of the engineering.
